summary: Set up a basic functional gun with client-side input and server-side damage handling, designed for beginners.

tasks.name: RemoteEvent Setup
tasks.description: Create a RemoteEvent named 'FireGunEvent' inside a folder named 'Events' in ReplicatedStorage. This will be the communication bridge for firing the gun.
tasks.result:
The provided `getOrCreateFolder` function has one critical bug and an incomplete line. Additionally, the script's overall purpose (setting up RemoteEvents) is not fully implemented in the provided snippet.

Here's a breakdown of the issues and the fixed, complete code:

**Bugs/Issues Found:**

1.  **Missing `return` statement in `getOrCreateFolder`**: At the end of the `getOrCreateFolder` function, the line `return newFolder` is commented out or incomplete (`-- Re`). If the folder needs to be created (either because it didn't exist or an incorrectly typed instance was destroyed), the function would currently return `nil`, which would lead to errors if the caller expects a `Folder` object.
2.  **Incomplete Script**: The script aims to set up RemoteEvents, but only provides a helper function for folders. It's missing the corresponding helper for `RemoteEvent` and the actual logic to call these helpers to set up `FireGunEvent`.

**Fixes Applied:**

1.  **Completed `return newFolder`**: The final `return newFolder` line in `getOrCreateFolder` has been uncommented/completed.
2.  **Added `getOrCreateRemoteEvent` function**: A new helper function, `getOrCreateRemoteEvent`, similar in robust design to `getOrCreateFolder`, has been added to safely create or retrieve `RemoteEvent` instances.
3.  **Added main setup logic**: Code has been added to actually call `getOrCreateFolder` and `getOrCreateRemoteEvent` to set up the "Events" folder and the "FireGunEvent" inside `ReplicatedStorage`.
4.  **Refined comments and output**: Adjusted some `print`/`warn` messages for clarity.

---

--!strict
-- EventSetup.lua
-- This script runs on the server to ensure that necessary RemoteEvents and their parent folders are set up
-- in ReplicatedStorage. This makes sure our client-server communication channels are ready.

-- Define the name of the specific RemoteEvent for firing our gun.
local FIRE_GUN_EVENT_NAME: string = "FireGunEvent"

-- Function to safely get or create a folder.
-- This prevents errors if the folder already exists and creates it if it doesn't.
-- It also includes robust input validation and handles cases where an existing child
-- has the correct name but an incorrect type.

        error(string.format("getOrCreateFolder: 'parent' must be an Instance, but got %s", typeof(parent)), 2)
    end
    -- Input validation for 'folderName'
    if not (typeof(folderName) == "string") or folderName:len() == 0 then
        error(string.format("getOrCr

tasks.name: Gun Client-Side Logic
tasks.description: Write a LocalScript to detect when the player equips the gun and clicks the mouse. It will play a sound and muzzle flash, then send a firing request to the server.
tasks.result:
Here's the final reviewed and fixed `ClientGunScript.lua`.

I've addressed the "ALIGNMENT REPORT FIX" comments and added robust error handling, component caching, input handling, animation control, and state management.

```lua
-- FILENAME: ClientGunScript.lua
-- This script handles the client-side logic for a gun, including input detection,
-- firing animations, sound, muzzle flash, and communicating with the server.

local Tool = script.Parent

--[[ CONFIGURATION ]]--
local CONFIG = {
    FIRE_RATE = 0.1, -- Time between shots in seconds (e.g., 0.1 for 10 shots/sec)
    MAX_AMMO = 30,   -- Max ammunition capacity (client-side only for display/logic)
    MUZZLE_FLASH_DURATION = 0.05, -- How long the muzzle flash is visible (for BasePart or ParticleEmitter)
    RAYCAST_MAX_DISTANCE = 500, -- Max distance for server-side raycast (for visual consistency)
    -- Add more config options as needed (e.g., reload time, damage, bullet speed)
}

--[[ ANIMATION IDS ]]--
local ANIMATION_IDS = {
    SHOOT = "rbxassetid://0", -- Placeholder: Replace with actual shoot animation ID
    -- Add other animation IDs as needed (e.g., reload, equip, idle)
}

--[[ SERVICES ]]--
local Players = game:GetService("Players")
local UserInputService = game:GetService("UserInputService")
local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")
local Debris = game:GetService("Debris") -- For managing temporary effects (e.g., hiding muzzle flash)
local RunService = game:GetService("RunService") -- For getting current camera/mouse data

--[[ PLAYER DATA ]]--
local LocalPlayer = Players.LocalPlayer
local Character = LocalPlayer.Character or LocalPlayer.CharacterAdded:Wait()
local Humanoid: Humanoid? = Character:FindFirstChildOfClass("Humanoid")
local Camera = workspace.CurrentCamera -- Always available in client. Access this directly in functions for freshest data.

--[[ REMOTE EVENT SETUP ]]--
-- ALIGNMENT REPORT FIX 3: Initialize FireGunEvent
local FireGunEvent: RemoteEvent? = ReplicatedStorage:FindFirstChild("RemoteEvents")?.FireGunEvent

if not FireGunEvent then
    warn("ClientGunScript: FireGunEvent not found in ReplicatedStorage/RemoteEvents! Firing functionality will be disabled.")
    -- Crucial event missing, consider disabling the gun or showing an error to the player.
    -- For now, firing attempts will simply fail gracefully.
end

--[[ GUN COMPONENTS (cached upon equip) ]]--
local GunHandle: Part? = nil
local ShootPart: Part? = nil -- The part where bullets originate and muzzle flash appears
local MuzzleFlash: Instance? = nil -- Visual effect for firing (e.g., a Part, MeshPart, or ParticleEmitter)
local ShootSound: Sound? = nil -- Sound effect for firing

--[[ ANIMATION TRACKS ]]--
local Animator: Animator? = nil
local ShootAnimationTrack: AnimationTrack? = nil

--[[ STATE VARIABLES ]]--
local isEquipped: boolean = false
local isFiringInputDown: boolean = false
local canFire: boolean = true -- Controls fire rate
local isReloading: boolean = false -- Placeholde

tasks.description: Develop a Script that listens for 'FireGunEvent' from the client. It will validate the firing request, perform a raycast from the player's camera, and apply damage to any hit character.
tasks.result:
```lua
--!strict

-- FILENAME: ServerGunLogic.lua

-- Define constants for our gun's behavior. Good practice to keep these at the top.
local DAMAGE = 25              -- How much damage this gun deals
local RAYCAST_LENGTH = 300     -- How far our raycast will go (range of the gun)
local FIRE_RATE_COOLDOWN = 0.2 -- How long (in seconds) a player must wait between shots
local HEADSHOT_MULTIPLIER = 2  -- Damage multiplier for headshots
local MAX_PING_TOLERANCE = 0.2 -- Max allowed ping difference for client-server time synchronization (advanced anti-exploit)
                               -- This is a buffer for network fluctuations and clock skew.

-- Get references to important Roblox services and objects.
local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")
local Players = game:GetService("Players")
local Workspace = game:GetService("Workspace")
local Debris = game:GetService("Debris") -- Used for temporary visual effects or cleanup (not directly used in this snippet but good to have)

-- Find our 'Events' folder and the 'FireGunEvent' within it.
-- We use FindFirstChild to safely access these, in case they're not there.
local eventsFolder = ReplicatedStorage:FindFirstChild("Events")
if not eventsFolder then
    warn("ERROR: Events folder not found in ReplicatedStorage! Please create it.")
    return -- Stop the script if the folder is missing
end

local fireGunEvent = eventsFolder:FindFirstChild("FireGunEvent")
if not fireGunEvent then
    warn("ERROR: FireGunEvent not found in ReplicatedStorage.Events! Please create it.")
    return -- Stop the script if the event is missing
end

-- A table to keep track of when each player can fire again.
-- This helps us implement the FIRE_RATE_COOLDOWN server-side.
-- Using os.clock() for better precision with fractional cooldowns, and typed for strictness.
local playerLastFired: {[number]: number} = {}

-- Utility function to get a player's character and humanoid safely.
-- @param player: The player whose character and humanoid to retrieve.
-- @return tuple: (Character Model, Humanoid) or (nil, nil) if not found or invalid.
local function getPlayerCharacterAndHumanoid(player: Player): (Model?, Humanoid?)
    local character = player.Character
    if not character or not character.Parent then
        return nil, nil
    end

    local humanoid = character:FindFirstChildOfClass("Humanoid")
    if not humanoid or humanoid.Health <= 0 then -- Check for health > 0 to ensure player is alive
        return nil, nil
    end

    return character, humanoid
end

original_request: make a flying player system with 1 task
summary: Develop a basic client-side flying system with input handling and physics manipulation.

tasks.name: Client Flying Script
tasks.description: Create a LocalScript to detect player input (e.g., key presses) to toggle and control player flight. This script will manipulate the player's HumanoidRootPart velocity/CFrame to achieve a flying effect.
tasks.result:
The provided code has several critical bugs and areas for improvement, particularly regarding input handling for simultaneous key presses and robust state management for character properties.

Here's a breakdown of the identified issues and the completely fixed code:

**Identified Bugs & Issues:**

1.  **Critical Syntax Error in `cleanup()`:**
    The line `i` within the `for` loop in `cleanup()` is incomplete and will cause a runtime error. It should be `connection:Disconnect()`.
2.  **Critical Logic Error in Input Handling (Simultaneous Movement):**
    The current approach for `handleInput` overwrites `currentMoveVector` and `currentVerticalMove` when multiple keys are pressed. For example, if 'W' and 'A' are pressed, only the last key pressed's effect will be registered. This prevents diagonal movement (WASD) and simultaneous up/down movement (Space + Ctrl). It needs to track the state of each relevant key individually.
3.  **Hardcoded `DEFAULT_JUMP_POWER`:**
    `DEFAULT_JUMP_POWER` is hardcoded to `50`. A game's default jump power might vary. It's best practice to capture the `Humanoid.JumpPower` dynamically from the character *before* flight begins and restore that specific value. The same applies to `WalkSpeed` for robustness, although `16` is a very common default.
4.  **Inconsistent `isFlying` state upon character setup:**
    The `setupCharacter` function calls `cleanup()` at the start, which sets `isFlying = false`. Consequently, the line `if isFlying then enableFlight() end` inside `setupCharacter` will never be true. This means a player will never automatically resume flight after respawning, even if they were flying before. While often a desired behavior (don't auto-fly on respawn), it's good to be aware of this explicit design choice. For now, I'll keep it as is, assuming respawning grounds the player is intended.

---

**Fixed Code:**

```lua
--!strict

--[[
    FlightClient.lua

    This LocalScript enables client-side flight for the player.
    It provides functionality to toggle flight, move horizontally and vertically using camera direction,
    and handles character respawns and cleanup gracefully.

    Features:
    - Customizable toggle key and flight speed.
    - Uses UserInputService for smooth and responsive input handling.
    - Utilizes RunService.Heartbeat for consistent physics updates.
    - Robust error handling for missing character components.
    - Optimizations for performance, especially within the Heartbeat loop.
    - Proper cleanup of connections to prevent memory leaks upon character respawn or removal.
    - Restores character's original properties (walk speed, jump power) upon exiting flight.
]]

-- Services: Essential Roblox APIs for client-side functionality.
local UserInputService = game:GetService("UserInputService")
local Players = game:GetService("Players")
local RunService = game:GetService("RunService")
local Workspace = game:GetService("Workspace")

-- Configuration variables: Easy to modify th

summary: Develop a simple client-side flying system for players, enabling them to toggle flight and control their character's movement in the air.

tasks.name: ClientFlyHandler
tasks.description: Create a LocalScript that detects player input to toggle flight mode, sets the character's Humanoid.PlatformStand property, and directly manipulates the HumanoidRootPart's CFrame for aerial movement.
tasks.result:
Alright, let's get this fly system cooked up. You want a client-side solution that toggles flight and handles movement directly. No sweat. This will get you soaring like a true Ashtrails'Studio dev.

Here's the lowdown: we'll use a `LocalScript` to pick up player input. When you hit the toggle key, `Humanoid.PlatformStand` goes `true` to defy gravity, and we'll take over movement by directly adjusting the `HumanoidRootPart`'s `CFrame`. WASD will control horizontal movement relative to your camera, and Space/Shift will handle vertical. Pretty slick, right?

Pay close attention to the comments; they're there to help you, the beginner, understand every line. This code is designed to be super clear.

FILENAME: ClientFlyHandler.lua
```lua
-- FILENAME: ClientFlyHandler.lua
-- This is a LocalScript. Place it in 'StarterPlayerScripts' within StarterPlayer.
-- This script enables client-side flight for the local player.

-- Services: These are essential parts of Roblox that we need to interact with.
local UserInputService = game:GetService("UserInputService") -- Used to detect keyboard and mouse input.
local RunService = game:GetService("RunService")             -- Used for running code every frame for smooth movement.
local Players = game:GetService("Players")                   -- Used to get the local player.

-- Player and Character References:
-- We need the LocalPlayer, their Character (the 3D model), its Humanoid (for health, walkspeed, etc.),
-- and the HumanoidRootPart (the main part of the character that moves everything).
local LocalPlayer = Players.LocalPlayer
local Character = LocalPlayer.Character or LocalPlayer.CharacterAdded:Wait() -- Get character, or wait for it to load.
local Humanoid = Character:WaitForChild("Humanoid")                         -- Get the Humanoid component.
local HumanoidRootPart = Character:WaitForChild("HumanoidRootPart")         -- Get the HumanoidRootPart.

-- Configuration (Constants):
-- These values can be easily changed to customize the flying experience.
local FLIGHT_TOGGLE_KEY = Enum.KeyCode.F    -- The key to press to turn flying ON or OFF.
local FLY_SPEED = 40                        -- How fast the player moves while flying.
local JUMP_KEY = Enum.KeyCode.Space         -- Key for moving upwards.
local SPRINT_KEY = Enum.KeyCode.LeftShift   -- Key for moving downwards.

-- State Variables:
-- These variables keep track of the current state of our flying system.
local isFlying = false                      -- True if the player is currently flying, false otherwise.
local connection_Stepped = nil              -- This will hold our connection to RunService.Stepped.
                                            -- We need to store it so we can disconnect it when not flying.

-- Input State Variables:
-- These help us know which directional keys are currently pressed.
-- Values: 1 (pressed forward/right/up), -1 (pressed backward/left/down), 0 (not pressed).
local inputForward = 0  -- Controls forward (W) / backward (S)

summary: A basic teleport system that allows players to teleport to a predefined location using a UI button, handled securely by the server.

tasks.name: TeleportServerHandler
tasks.description: Create a server script in ServerScriptService. This script will set up a RemoteEvent in ReplicatedStorage if it doesn't exist, listen for client requests via that event, validate the player, and then teleport them to a specific location.
tasks.result:
Here's the reviewed and fixed code.

I found two primary bugs and one minor cosmetic issue:

1.  **Bug 1: `waitForCharacter` does not respect `timeout` and can yield indefinitely.** The original `player.CharacterAdded:Wait()` call does not have a timeout mechanism. If a player's character fails to load for an extended period, this function would never return, potentially stalling the teleport request handler.
2.  **Bug 2: Redundant and slightly flawed player validation in `OnServerEvent`.** The `player` object provided by `RemoteEvent.OnServerEvent` is always a valid `Player` instance. Checking `Players:FindFirstChild(player.Name)` is unnecessary and can be misleading if player names are manipulated (though `player` object itself remains trusted).
3.  **Minor Issue: Incomplete comment.** A comment in `onPlayerRemoving` was cut off.

---

```lua
-- FILENAME: TeleportServerHandler.lua

-- This script lives in ServerScriptService and handles player teleportation requests from the client.
-- It sets up a RemoteEvent and listens for signals to securely move players to a predefined location.
-- This version includes robust error handling, input validation, rate limiting, and improved structure.

-- region -- Core Services --
local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")
local Players = game:GetService("Players")
-- local RunService = game:GetService("RunService") -- RunService is not used in this script, can be removed.
-- endregion

-- region -- Configuration Constants --
-- The name of the RemoteEvent. Both client and server must agree on this name.
-- Using a constant (uppercase name) makes it easy to find and change later.
local TELEPORT_EVENT_NAME: string = "TeleportRequest"

-- The fixed destination where players will be teleported.
-- Using CFrame for both position and orientation. Placing slightly above Y=0 to avoid clipping.
local TELEPORT_DESTINATION: CFrame = CFrame.new(0, 50, 0)

-- Cooldown period (in seconds) to prevent players from spamming teleport requests.
local TELEPORT_COOLDOWN_SECONDS: number = 3

-- Maximum number of attempts to teleport a character if the initial try fails.
local MAX_TELEPORT_ATTEMPTS: number = 3

-- Delay (in seconds) between teleport attempts for retries.
local RETRY_DELAY_SECONDS: number = 0.1

-- Maximum time (in seconds) to wait for a player's character to load.
local CHARACTER_LOAD_TIMEOUT: number = 5
-- endregion

-- region -- Module State --
-- Table to store the last successful teleport time for each player, used for rate limiting.
-- Keys are Player objects, values are os.time() timestamps.
local lastTeleportTime: {[Player]: number} = {}
-- endregion

-- region -- RemoteEvent Setup --
-- Find or create the RemoteEvent in ReplicatedStorage.
-- ReplicatedStorage is accessible by both server and client, making it the perfect place for RemoteEvents.
local teleportEvent: RemoteEvent = ReplicatedStorage:FindFirstChild(TELEPORT_EVENT_NAME)

if not teleportEvent then
    -- If the RemoteEvent d

tasks.name: TeleportClientUI
tasks.description: Create a client script in StarterPlayerScripts. This script will generate a ScreenGui with a 'Teleport' button. When the button is clicked, it will fire the RemoteEvent created by the server script to request teleportation.
tasks.result:
The provided script has one critical bug related to a truncated RemoteEvent name and can be made more robust in handling `LocalPlayer` availability.

Here's a breakdown of the fixes and improvements:

1.  **Critical Bug Fix**: The `getRemoteEvent` call for `"TeleportToTargetEven"` was truncated. It has been corrected to `"TeleportToTargetEvent"`.
2.  **Robust `LocalPlayer` Initialization**: Client scripts should always ensure `Players.LocalPlayer` is available before attempting to interact with player-specific services (like `PlayerGui`) or call `StarterGui:SetCore`. The script now uses `local LocalPlayer = Players.LocalPlayer or Players.LocalPlayer:Wait()` at the top, which guarantees `LocalPlayer` is valid before the rest of the script executes. This removes the need for `LocalPlayer` checks within every function and simplifies the main execution block.
3.  **Improved Error Feedback**: If the `TeleportToTargetEvent` RemoteEvent cannot be found, the script now visually indicates this by changing the Teleport button's text and background color, and disabling it. This provides better feedback to the user and developer.

Here is the complete fixed code:

```lua
local Players = game:GetService("Players")
local StarterGui = game:GetService("StarterGui")
local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")

-- Ensure LocalPlayer is available before proceeding with the rest of the script.
-- This is the most robust way to get LocalPlayer in a client script, ensuring it's
-- not nil before any subsequent code that relies on it.
local LocalPlayer = Players.LocalPlayer or Players.LocalPlayer:Wait()

-- Define a constant for the UI name to prevent duplicates
local GUI_NAME = "TeleportScreenGui"

-- Function to log errors to the console and notify the user
local function logError(message: string)
    warn("[TeleportClientScript Error]: " .. message)
    StarterGui:SetCore("SendNotification", {
        Title = "Error",
        Text = message,
        Duration = 5,
        Button1 = "OK"
    })
end

-- Function to safely get a RemoteEvent from ReplicatedStorage
-- Type annotations use built-in Roblox types, not custom aliases
local function getRemoteEvent(eventName: string): RemoteEvent?
    local remoteEvent = ReplicatedStorage:WaitForChild(eventName, 10) -- Wait up to 10 seconds for the event
    if not remoteEvent then
        logError(string.format("Failed to find RemoteEvent '%s' in ReplicatedStorage within the timeout.", eventName))
        return nil
    end
    -- Robustness check: ensure the found instance is actually a RemoteEvent
    if not remoteEvent:IsA("RemoteEvent") then
        logError(string.format("Found '%s' in ReplicatedStorage, but it is not a RemoteEvent.", eventName))
        return nil
    end
    return remoteEvent
end

-- Main function to create the UI and set up event listeners
local function createUIAndSetupEvents()
    -- LocalPlayer is guaranteed to be available here due to the Players.LocalPlayer:Wait() at the top.

summary: Generate a server script to create a touch-activated teleport part.

tasks.name: TeleportPartScript
tasks.description: Create a Server Script that will be placed inside a Part. This script will detect when a player's character touches the part and then teleport that character to a predefined target location. It should include basic debounce to prevent spamming.
tasks.result:
Alright, let's get this teleport pad engineered! You want a solid, reliable teleport system for your game, and that's precisely what we'll build. No janky debounce issues or missed teleports allowed on my watch. This script is designed to be dropped right into your `Part` object in Roblox Studio.

Here’s the server script, crafted with precision and optimal performance in mind. Remember, keep those server scripts in `ServerScriptService` or parented directly to the objects they manage, never in `StarterPlayerScripts` if they handle core game logic.

```lua
FILENAME: TeleportPartScript.lua
--[[
    TeleportPartScript.lua

    This script manages a touch-activated teleport functionality for a Roblox Part.
    When a player's character touches the parent part of this script, they will be
    teleported to a predefined target location. Includes a robust debounce
    mechanism to prevent rapid, unintended teleports.

    Place this script directly inside the Part you want to act as the teleport pad.

    Ashtrails'Studio — Engineered for Excellence.
]]

-- Get a reference to the Part this script is parented to.
-- This makes the script reusable for any part you want to make a teleport pad.
local TeleportPart = script.Parent

-- Define the target destination for the teleport.
-- !! IMPORTANT !!
-- Change these coordinates (X, Y, Z) to the actual location where you want players
-- to be teleported. Make sure it's above ground or in a safe spot!
-- Example: Vector3.new(0, 50, 0) would teleport players to the world origin at Y=50.
local TARGET_POSITION: Vector3 = Vector3.new(50, 10, 50) -- <--- ADJUST THIS TO YOUR DESIRED LOCATION!

-- Debounce variable to prevent spamming the teleport.
-- When a player teleports, this becomes true, blocking further teleports until
-- the debounce time passes.
local debounce: boolean = false

-- The duration (in seconds) for which the debounce will be active.
-- Players must wait this long before they can teleport again using this part.
local DEBOUNCE_TIME: number = 2 -- 2 seconds cooldown.

-- A local function to handle what happens when something touches our TeleportPart.
-- The 'hit' parameter is the part that touched our TeleportPart.
local function onPartTouched(hit: BasePart)
    -- Check if the debounce is currently active. If it is, we exit early
    -- to prevent multiple teleports from a single touch or rapid touches.
    if debounce then
        return
    end

    -- Attempt to find the character model from the 'hit' part.
    -- The 'hit' part could be a limb (e.g., 'LeftFoot') of a player's character.
    local character: Model? = hit.Parent
    -- If there's no parent, or the parent isn't a Model, it's likely not a character.
    if not character or not character:IsA("Model") then
        return
    end

    -- Look for a Humanoid within the character model.
    -- The Humanoid is crucial for identifying a player's avatar and checking their state.
    local humanoid: Humanoid? = character:FindFirstChildOfCl

original_request: Make a command in Roblox that makes player flys
summary: Develop a server-sided chat command ('/fly', '/unfly') to toggle a client-controlled player flight system, including custom movement controls.

tasks.name: Server Fly Command Handler
tasks.description: Create a server script in ServerScriptService. This script will create a RemoteEvent in ReplicatedStorage if it doesn't exist. It listens for chat commands '/fly' and '/unfly', toggles the player's Humanoid.PlatformStand and HumanoidRootPart.CanCollide properties on the server, and fires the RemoteEvent to the respective client to activate or deactivate their flight controller.
tasks.result:
Alright, let's get this done. You're looking to build a robust flight system, and starting with a solid server-side command handler is a smart move. We'll set up a `RemoteEvent` and get that chat command humming. This is standard operating procedure for building engaging mechanics in the Swrift project, where performance and scalability are king.

Here’s the complete Luau code for Task 1, following all the best practices we swear by at Ashtrails'Studio. Pay close attention to the comments – they're there to help you understand every line, especially since you're new to this.

FILENAME: `FlyCommandHandler.lua`
```lua
--!strict

-- Services we'll be using. Always get services this way.
local Players = game:GetService("Players")
local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")
local ServerScriptService = game:GetService("ServerScriptService")

-- Define the name of our RemoteEvent. Consistency is key, folks.
local FLY_EVENT_NAME = "FlyEvent"

-- Function to get or create the RemoteEvent.
-- This ensures our client-side scripts always have a RemoteEvent to listen to.
local function getOrCreateFlyEvent(): RemoteEvent
    local flyEvent = ReplicatedStorage:FindFirstChild(FLY_EVENT_NAME)
    if not flyEvent then
        -- If it doesn't exist, create a new RemoteEvent.
        flyEvent = Instance.new("RemoteEvent")
        flyEvent.Name = FLY_EVENT_NAME
        flyEvent.Parent = ReplicatedStorage -- Put it where clients can access it.
        warn("Created new FlyEvent in ReplicatedStorage. Always check for existing instances first!")
    end
    return flyEvent
end

-- Get our RemoteEvent.
local flyEvent = getOrCreateFlyEvent()

-- Function to handle the player's flight state.
-- This is where the magic happens on the server.
local function setPlayerFlightState(player: Player, enableFlight: boolean)
    local character = player.Character
    local humanoid = character and character:FindFirstChildOfClass("Humanoid")
    local humanoidRootPart = character and character:FindFirstChild("HumanoidRootPart")

    -- Always nil-check your instances, especially when dealing with player characters.
    -- Characters can respawn, and parts might not exist immediately.
    if not character or not humanoid or not humanoidRootPart then
        warn("Could not find character, humanoid, or HumanoidRootPart for player " .. player.Name .. ". Cannot set flight state.")
        return
    end

    -- Server-side properties to prevent falling and allow custom movement.
    humanoid.PlatformStand = enableFlight
    humanoidRootPart.CanCollide = not enableFlight -- Turn off collision when flying.

    -- Fire the RemoteEvent to the specific client.
    -- This tells their local script whether to activate or deactivate flight controls.
    -- We're sending a single boolean: true for 'fly', false for 'unfly'.
    local success, errorMessage = pcall(function()
        flyEvent:FireClient(player, enableFlight)
    end)

    if not success then
        warn("Failed

tasks.name: Client Fly Movement Controller
tasks.description: Create a local script in StarterPlayerScripts. This script listens for the RemoteEvent from the server. When flying is enabled, it disables default Roblox movement scripts and uses UserInputService to capture WASD, Space, and Shift key presses to directly manipulate the player's character CFrame, allowing for free-form flight. When disabled, it re-enables default movement.
tasks.result:
Alright, let's get this client-side flight controller cooked up. We're talking pure Luau magic here, making players soar like it's no biggie. This script will live in `StarterPlayerScripts`, pick up the signal from the server, and then unleash a custom movement system that feels smoother than 60 FPS on a 3080.

Remember, Ashtrails'Studio crafts experiences, not just games. This is how we push the envelope.

Here’s the breakdown for the client-side flight system:

1.  **Event Listener:** We'll set up a listener for the `FlyEvent` `RemoteEvent` in `ReplicatedStorage`.
2.  **Input Handling:** `UserInputService` will capture key presses (WASD for directional, Space for up, Shift for down).
3.  **Flight Loop:** `RunService.RenderStepped` will be our engine, constantly updating the player's `HumanoidRootPart.CFrame` for buttery-smooth flight.
4.  **State Management:** We'll toggle flight controls on and off, making sure to clean up connections and restore default movement when needed.

This script ensures a seamless flight experience while adhering to top-tier Luau practices.

FILENAME: FlyClientController.lua

```lua
--!strict

-- Table to hold active connections, so we can easily disconnect them

-- Function to get character components safely
local function getCharacterComponents()
    character = localPlayer.Character
    if not character then
        -- Character might not be loaded yet or respawned
        character = localPlayer.CharacterAdded:Wait()
    end
    humanoid = character:FindFirstChildOfClass("Humanoid")
    humanoidRootPart = character:FindFirstChild("HumanoidRootPart")

    if not humanoid or not humanoidRootPart then
        warn("FlyClientController: Missing Humanoid or HumanoidRootPart in character.")
        return false
    end
    return true
end

summary: Develop a server script to make a specified part detect and actively flee from the closest player, adjusting its movement direction dynamically.

tasks.name: FleeingPartController
tasks.description: Create a server script that initializes a special part, then continuously monitors for the closest player within a defined radius. If a player is detected, the part calculates a safe direction and moves away from that player using CFrame manipulation. Includes configurable speed and detection range.
tasks.result:
Alright, let's get this FleeingPartController cooked up. You want a part that's got some serious "nope" energy when a player gets too close, darting away like it just saw a bug in production. I got you.

This server script will make sure your part *actually* flees, not just sits there, while keeping that game-dev grind smooth. We'll use `CFrame` manipulation for precise movement, because who needs physics when you're a digital escape artist?

```lua
-- FILENAME: FleeingPartController.lua
-- This script runs on the server to control a part that flees from nearby players.
-- It's robust, well-commented, and follows Ashtrails'Studio's code quality standards.

-- Services
local Workspace = game:GetService("Workspace")
local Players = game:GetService("Players")

-- Configuration
-- The name of the part in Workspace that this script will control.
-- Make sure this part exists and is anchored if you want smooth CFrame movement!
local PART_NAME: string = "FleeingCube" -- <--- Change this to your part's name!
local FLEE_SPEED: number = 25 -- How fast the part moves away (studs per second). Adjust as needed.
local DETECTION_RADIUS: number = 50 -- How close a player needs to be for the part to start fleeing (studs).
local UPDATE_INTERVAL: number = 0.1 -- How often the script checks for players and updates the part's position (seconds).

-- Variables to store the actual part instance
local fleeingPart: Part = nil

-- Function to initialize the script and find the part
local function setupFleeingPart()
    -- Attempt to find the part in the Workspace.
    -- Using FindFirstChild for nil-checking, because we don't want runtime errors if the part isn't there.
    local foundPart = Workspace:FindFirstChild(PART_NAME)

    if foundPart and foundPart:IsA("BasePart") then
        fleeingPart = foundPart
        -- Ensure the part is anchored for CFrame movement to work predictably without physics interference.
        fleeingPart.Anchored = true
        print(string.format("Ashtrails'Studio: Fleeing part '%s' found and ready to get its flee on!", PART_NAME))
    else
        warn(string.format("Ashtrails'Studio: Fleeing part '%s' not found or is not a BasePart in Workspace! Script will not run.", PART_NAME))
        return false -- Indicate failure
    end
    return true -- Indicate success
end

-- Function to find the closest player to the fleeing part within the detection radius
-- @param currentFleeingPart: Part -- The part that is fleeing.
-- @return Player | nil -- The closest player, or nil if no player is found within range.
local function findClosestPlayer(currentFleeingPart: Part): Player
    local closestPlayer: Player = nil
    local minDistance: number = DETECTION_RADIUS + 1 -- Initialize with a value outside the detection range

    -- Loop through all currently connected players
    for _, player in ipairs(Players:GetPlayers()) do
        -- Ensure the player has a character and a HumanoidRootPart (which is essential for position)
        local chara

summary: Develops a comprehensive level and experience system, including server-side logic, data persistence, leaderstat integration, and a configurable progression curve.

tasks.name: LevelSystemConfig
tasks.description: Create a ModuleScript to define the experience curve for each level, initial player stats, and other system-wide configurations.
tasks.result:
Alright, let's get this config module cranked out. You need a solid foundation for that level system, and a ModuleScript is the absolute move for keeping things clean and configurable. We'll define the XP curve, initial stats, and all the juicy bits your `LevelSystemService` will need.

I've crafted a `LevelSystemConfig` module that's got explicit XP requirements for early levels and then smoothly transitions into a configurable formula for the grind to the top. This ensures your game's progression feels tuned, not just thrown together.

Here's the code. Drop this bad boy into `ReplicatedStorage` (or wherever your server scripts can `require` it) as `LevelSystemConfig.lua`.

FILENAME: LevelSystemConfig.lua
```lua
--!strict
--[[
    @Module LevelSystemConfig
    @Client & @Server

    Ashtrails'Studio LevelSystemConfig Module
    -------------------------------------------
    This module centralizes all configurable parameters for the game's level and experience system.
    It defines:
    - DataStore keys for persistence.
    - Leaderstat names for player display.
    - Initial stats for new players.
    - The experience curve: explicit values for early levels, and a scalable formula for higher levels.

    Keeping these values in one place makes balancing and updates a breeze.
    Think of it as the ultimate cheat sheet for your game's progression.
]]

local LevelSystemConfig = {}

-- Configuration for DataStore access
-- This key ensures unique storage for player level data. Update the version number (`v1`, `v2`, etc.)
-- if you make breaking changes to the data structure, to avoid conflicts with old saves.
LevelSystemConfig.DataStoreKey = "PlayerLevelData_v1"

-- Configuration for leaderstats display
-- These are the exact names that will appear in the player list.
LevelSystemConfig.LeaderstatNames = {
    Level = "Level",     -- Display name for the player's current level
    Experience = "XP",   -- Display name for the player's current experience points
}

-- Initial player statistics for new players joining the game for the first time.
LevelSystemConfig.InitialStats = {
    Level = 1,
    Experience = 0,
}

-- Maximum level a player can achieve in the game.
-- Once this level is reached, no more experience is needed.
LevelSystemConfig.MaxLevel = 100

-- Experience Curve Definition
-- This table maps a current level (key) to the total experience points (value)
-- required to advance to the *next* level.
-- Example: XP_NEEDED_PER_LEVEL[1] is the XP required to reach Level 2 from Level 1.
-- This allows for precise tuning of early-game progression.
LevelSystemConfig.XP_NEEDED_PER_LEVEL = {
    [1] = 100,    -- To reach Level 2
    [2] = 250,    -- To reach Level 3
    [3] = 500,    -- To reach Level 4
    [4] = 900,    -- To reach Level 5
    [5] = 1500,   -- To reach Level 6
    [6] = 2300,   -- To reach Level 7
    [7] = 3300,   -- To reach Level 8
    [8] = 4500,   -- To reach Level 9
    [9] = 6000,   -- To reach Level 10
    [10] = 8000,

tasks.name: LevelSystemService
tasks.description: Implement the core server-side logic, including DataStore management for player levels and experience, leaderstat updates, functions for adding experience, and handling level-up events. This will ensure persistent progression and real-time stat display.
tasks.result:
Alright, let's dive deep into the core server-side logic for our leveling system. This `LevelSystemService` is where the magic happens, managing player data, dishing out XP, and orchestrating those epic level-up moments. We're going to build it robustly, with data persistence and performance in mind.

This script will live in `ServerScriptService` and will handle:
*   Loading and saving player level and XP data using `DataStoreService`.
*   Setting up and updating `leaderstats` so everyone can flex their progress.
*   A powerful `AddExperience` function that manages XP gain, level-ups, and even cap-offs at max level.
*   Error handling for all our DataStore calls, because data integrity is non-negotiable.

Remember, this system hooks into the `LevelSystemConfig` module we set up in Task 1, pulling in all the XP curve and initial settings. If you haven't nailed down that config yet, make sure it's squared away!

Here's the code. Drop this bad boy into `ServerScriptService` and name it `LevelSystemService.lua`.

FILENAME: LevelSystemService.lua
```lua
--!strict
--[[
    LevelSystemService.lua
    Ashtrails'Studio - Lead Digital Architect

    Description:
        Core server-side logic for player level and experience progression.
        Manages DataStore operations, leaderstat updates, adding experience,
        and handling level-up events.

    Dependencies:
        - ServerScriptService.LevelSystemConfig (ModuleScript from Task 1)
        - ReplicatedStorage.LevelUpRemote (RemoteEvent, optional for client UI updates)
]]

local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")
local ServerScriptService = game:GetService("ServerScriptService")
local DataStoreService = game:GetService("DataStoreService")
local Players = game:GetService("Players")

-- Load the LevelSystemConfig module
local LevelSystemConfig: {
    INITIAL_LEVEL: number,
    INITIAL_XP: number,
    MAX_LEVEL: number,
    GetRequiredXP: (self: any, level: number) -> number
} = require(ServerScriptService:WaitForChild("LevelSystemConfig"))

-- Initialize DataStore
local PLAYER_LEVEL_DATASTORE_KEY = "PlayerLevelData_V1" -- Increment version if data structure changes
local playerLevelDataStore = DataStoreService:GetDataStore(PLAYER_LEVEL_DATASTORE_KEY)

-- RemoteEvent for notifying clients about level-ups (Optional, but good for UI)
local LevelUpRemote = ReplicatedStorage:FindFirstChild("LevelUpRemote")
if not LevelUpRemote then
    warn("[LevelSystemService] LevelUpRemote not found in ReplicatedStorage. Client UI updates for level-ups may not function.")
    -- You might want to create this RemoteEvent manually or programmatically if it's crucial.
    -- Example: LevelUpRemote = Instance.new("RemoteEvent"); LevelUpRemote.Name = "LevelUpRemote"; LevelUpRemote.Parent = ReplicatedStorage
end

-- Cache for player data (in-memory for quick access during gameplay)
local playerData: {[number]: {Level: number, XP: number, XPToNextLevel: number}} = {}

--[[
    @private GetPlayerData

summary: A simple teleport system with teleport pads that move players between locations. Includes server script for handling teleports and a configuration module for defining teleport points.

tasks.name: TeleportConfig
tasks.description: A configuration module that stores all teleport pad names, their destination positions, and optional cooldown settings. Placed in ReplicatedStorage.
tasks.result:
# Task 1: TeleportConfig Module

Alright, let's kick this off with the foundation — the config module. This is where you define all your teleport pads: what they're called in the workspace, where they send players, and how long the cooldown is before they can teleport again.

Think of this as the "brain" of your teleport system. The server script (Task 2) will read from this module to know what to do.

**Place this ModuleScript inside `ReplicatedStorage`.**

FILENAME: TeleportConfig.lua

```lua
--[[
	TeleportConfig Module
	=====================
	This module holds all the settings for our teleport system.
	
	HOW TO USE:
	1. Place this ModuleScript in ReplicatedStorage
	2. Name it "TeleportConfig"
	3. In the Workspace, create Parts and name them EXACTLY
	   as the keys in the "pads" table below (e.g., "TeleportPad_Spawn")
	4. Set the "destination" to the Vector3 position you want
	   the player to land at when they step on that pad.
	
	TIP: To find a position in Roblox Studio, move your cursor
	over the 3D view and look at the bottom bar for coordinates,
	or select a part and check its Position in Properties.
	
	Made with 🖤 at Ashtrails'Studio
--]]

-- Create the module table that we will return at the end
local TeleportConfig = {}

--[[
	DEFAULT COOLDOWN (in seconds)
	This is how long a player must wait before they can use
	ANY teleport pad again after teleporting.
	Individual pads can override this with their own cooldown.
--]]
TeleportConfig.defaultCooldown = 3

--[[
	TELEPORT PADS TABLE
	Each entry represents one teleport pad in your game.
	
	Structure:
	  ["PadNameInWorkspace"] = {
	      destination = Vector3.new(x, y, z),  -- where the player goes
	      cooldown = number (optional),         -- override default cooldown
	  }
	
	IMPORTANT:
	- The key (e.g., "TeleportPad_Spawn") must EXACTLY match
	  the name of the Part in Workspace.
	- The destination Y value should be a few studs above the ground
	  so the player doesn't get stuck inside the floor.
	  (Add about 3-5 studs above ground level)
--]]
TeleportConfig.pads = {

	-- Teleport Pad 1: Takes the player to the Shop area
	["TeleportPad_ToShop"] = {
		destination = Vector3.new(50, 5, 100),  -- Shop area position
		cooldown = 3,                            -- 3 second cooldown
	},

	-- Teleport Pad 2: Takes the player back to Spawn
	["TeleportPad_ToSpawn"] = {
		destination = Vector3.new(0, 5, 0),     -- Spawn area position
		cooldown = 3,                            -- 3 second cooldown
	},

	-- Teleport Pad 3: Takes the player to a Battle Arena
	["TeleportPad_ToArena"] = {
		destination = Vector3.new(-120, 5, 200), -- Arena position
		cooldown = 5,                             -- 5 second cooldown (longer!)
	},

	-- Teleport Pad 4: Takes the player to a Secret Room
	["TeleportPad_ToSecret"] = {
		destination = Vector3.new(300, 50, -80), -- Secret room (high up!)
		cooldown = 10,                            -- 10 second cooldown
	},

	--[[
		ADDING YOUR OWN PADS:
		Copy the template below,

tasks.name: TeleportServer
tasks.description: Server script that detects when a player touches a teleport pad, checks cooldown, and moves their character to the destination position. Handles cleanup on player leaving. Placed in ServerScriptService.
tasks.result:
# Task 2: TeleportServer 🚀

Alright, let's build the brain of your teleport system — the server script that actually moves players when they step on a pad.

Here's what this script does:
- **Loads your TeleportConfig** from Task 1
- **Listens for touch events** on every teleport pad defined in your config
- **Checks cooldowns** so players can't spam-teleport
- **Moves the player's character** to the destination position
- **Fires a RemoteEvent** to the client so Task 3 can play the fade effect
- **Cleans up everything** when a player leaves (no memory leaks!)

```lua
FILENAME: TeleportServer.lua
-- ============================================
-- TeleportServer.lua
-- Location: ServerScriptService
-- Purpose: Handles all teleport pad logic on the server
-- ============================================

-- SERVICES
-- We grab the services we need from Roblox
local Players = game:GetService("Players")
local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")

-- ============================================
-- LOAD THE CONFIG MODULE (from Task 1)
-- ============================================
-- This is the module you made in Task 1 that has all the pad info
local TeleportConfig = require(ReplicatedStorage:WaitForChild("TeleportConfig"))

-- ============================================
-- CREATE A REMOTE EVENT
-- ============================================
-- This RemoteEvent tells the CLIENT to play the fade effect (Task 3 will listen for this)
-- We create it here on the server so it always exists
local teleportEvent = Instance.new("RemoteEvent")
teleportEvent.Name = "TeleportEffect"       -- Give it a clear name
teleportEvent.Parent = ReplicatedStorage     -- Put it where client can access it

-- ============================================
-- COOLDOWN TRACKING
-- ============================================
-- We store cooldowns per player so they can't spam teleport pads
-- Structure: { [playerUserId] = { [padName] = lastTeleportTime } }
local playerCooldowns = {}

-- ============================================
-- HELPER FUNCTION: Check if a player is on cooldown
-- ============================================
-- Returns true if the player CAN teleport (not on cooldown)
-- Returns false if they need to wait
local function canTeleport(player, padName, cooldownTime)
	-- Get this player's cooldown table
	local userId = player.UserId
	local cooldowns = playerCooldowns[userId]

	-- If no cooldown table exists yet, they've never teleported — allow it
	if not cooldowns then
		return true
	end

	-- If they've never used THIS specific pad, allow it
	if not cooldowns[padName] then
		return true
	end

	-- Check if enough time has passed since their last teleport on this pad
	local lastTime = cooldowns[padName]
	local currentTime = tick() -- tick() gives us the current time in seconds

	-- If the difference is bigger than the cooldown, they can teleport again
	if (currentTime - lastTime) >= cooldownTime then
		return true
	end

	-- Still on cooldow

tasks.name: TeleportEffects
tasks.description: Client-side LocalScript that plays a visual effect (screen fade to black and back) when the player is teleported, giving a smooth transition feel. Placed in StarterPlayerScripts.
tasks.result:
# Task 3: TeleportEffects 🎬

Alright, final piece of the puzzle! This LocalScript creates a smooth **fade to black and back** whenever the player gets teleported. It listens for a RemoteEvent from the server so we know exactly when to play the effect.

**But first** — we need to make a small addition to the previous scripts so the server can *tell* the client when a teleport happens. I'll note that below, then give you the full client effect script.

---

## Quick Server-Side Addition

You need to create a **RemoteEvent** called `TeleportEffect` inside `ReplicatedStorage`. The server script (Task 2) needs to fire this event to the player right **before** teleporting them. Add this one line in your `TeleportServer` script, right before you set the character's CFrame:

```lua
-- Add this near the top of TeleportServer, with your other variables:
local teleportEffect = Instance.new("RemoteEvent")
teleportEffect.Name = "TeleportEffect"
teleportEffect.Parent = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")

-- Then, right BEFORE the line that moves the character (humanoidRootPart.CFrame = ...),
-- add this line:
teleportEffect:FireClient(player)
```

That way the client knows when to start the fade. Now here's the full effects script:

---

FILENAME: TeleportEffects.lua

```lua
--[[
	TeleportEffects (LocalScript)
	Location: StarterPlayerScripts

	What this does:
	- Listens for the server to tell us "hey, you just teleported!"
	- When that happens, fades the screen to black, waits a moment,
	  then fades back to normal
	- Uses a ScreenGui with a black Frame and TweenService for smooth animation

	This gives players a nice cinematic feel instead of just
	instantly popping to a new location.
]]

-- ============================================
-- SERVICES
-- We grab all the Roblox services we need
-- ============================================

local Players = game:GetService("Players")                   -- To get our local player
local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage") -- Where the RemoteEvent lives
local TweenService = game:GetService("TweenService")         -- For smooth fade animations

-- ============================================
-- PLAYER REFERENCES
-- ============================================

-- "LocalPlayer" is the player running THIS client
local localPlayer = Players.LocalPlayer

-- PlayerGui is where we put our screen effects
-- We wait for it to exist (it should always be there, but just to be safe)
local playerGui = localPlayer:WaitForChild("PlayerGui")

-- ============================================
-- SETTINGS (easy to change!)
-- ============================================

local FADE_IN_TIME = 0.4    -- How many seconds to go from clear -> black
local HOLD_TIME = 0.3       -- How many seconds to stay fully black
local FADE_OUT_TIME = 0.4   -- How many seconds to go from black -> clear
local FADE_COLOR = Color3.fromRGB(0, 0, 0)  -- Black color for the fade

-- ============================================
-- CREATE TH

summary: Complete gun system with raycast shooting, damage, ammo, reloading, and visual/audio feedback. Client handles input and FX, server handles damage and validation.

tasks.name: GunConfig
tasks.description: Shared configuration module: damage, fire rate, max ammo, reload time, range, spread, bullet speed, sounds, and visual settings.
tasks.result:
# Task 1: GunConfig — Shared Configuration Module

Alright, let's lay the foundation. Every solid gun system starts with a clean, centralized config module. This is the single source of truth — both the client and server will read from this, so nobody's hardcoding magic numbers scattered across scripts. Clean architecture from the jump.

This goes in **ReplicatedStorage** so both client and server can require it.

FILENAME: GunConfig.lua

```lua
--[[
	GunConfig.lua
	Shared configuration module for the gun system.
	Location: ReplicatedStorage/GunConfig
	
	Both the client (GunClient) and server (GunServer) require this module
	to stay in sync on all gun parameters. Change values HERE, not in
	individual scripts.
	
	Part of the Swrift project by Ashtrails'Studio.
--]]

local GunConfig = {}

------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- DAMAGE & COMBAT
------------------------------------------------------------------------

-- Base damage per bullet hit
GunConfig.Damage = 25

-- Headshot multiplier (head hit = Damage * HeadshotMultiplier)
GunConfig.HeadshotMultiplier = 2.0

-- Minimum damage at max range (falloff). Set equal to Damage to disable falloff.
GunConfig.MinDamage = 10

-- Distance (studs) at which damage starts falling off
GunConfig.DamageFalloffStart = 80

------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- FIRE RATE & SHOOTING
------------------------------------------------------------------------

-- Minimum seconds between each shot (0.1 = 10 shots/sec, 0.15 = ~6.67 shots/sec)
GunConfig.FireRate = 0.12

-- Maximum distance (studs) a bullet can travel
GunConfig.MaxRange = 300

-- Spread in degrees — 0 = perfectly accurate, higher = less accurate
-- Spread is applied as a random cone angle from the aim direction
GunConfig.BaseSpread = 1.5

-- Additional spread added per consecutive shot (recoil bloom)
GunConfig.SpreadIncreasePerShot = 0.3

-- Maximum total spread (base + bloom cap)
GunConfig.MaxSpread = 6.0

-- How fast spread recovers back to base (degrees per second)
GunConfig.SpreadRecoveryRate = 8.0

------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- AMMO & RELOADING
------------------------------------------------------------------------

-- Bullets in a full magazine
GunConfig.MaxAmmo = 30

-- Time in seconds to complete a reload
GunConfig.ReloadTime = 2.0

-- Whether the player can interrupt a reload by firing (true = cancel reload to shoot)
GunConfig.CanCancelReload = false

------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- RAYCAST SETTINGS
------------------------------------------------------------------------

-- RaycastFilterType used for bullet raycasts
-- We exclude the shooter's character so they don't shoot themselves
GunConfig.RaycastFilterType = Enum.RaycastFilterType.Exclude

-- Additional parts/models to add to the raycast filter (populated at runtime)
-- This is a default empty table; scripts will add to it as n

tasks.name: GunServer
tasks.description: Server script: handle RemoteEvent fire requests, validate cooldowns, perform server-side raycast, apply damage, replicate hit FX to all clients.
tasks.result:
# Task 2: GunServer — Server-Side Fire Handling

This is the brain of the gun system. Every shot request from a client gets validated here — no trusting the client, ever. The server checks cooldowns, performs its own raycast, applies damage, and tells all clients to show hit effects.

Here's what this script handles:
- **RemoteEvent setup** — Creates `FireGun` and `HitEffect` remotes
- **Cooldown validation** — Prevents fire-rate exploits with per-player tracking
- **Server-side raycast** — The server does the actual hit detection (never trust client raycasts for damage)
- **Damage application** — Finds the humanoid, applies damage, checks for kills
- **FX replication** — Fires a remote to ALL clients so everyone sees the hit effects
- **Cleanup** — Wipes player data on leave to prevent memory leaks

```lua
FILENAME: GunServer.lua

--[[
    GunServer.lua
    Location: ServerScriptService

    Server-side gun handler. Validates all fire requests,
    performs authoritative raycasts, applies damage, and
    replicates visual effects to all clients.

    Part of the Swrift Gun System by Ashtrails'Studio.
]]

-- Services
local Players = game:GetService("Players")
local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")
local ServerStorage = game:GetService("ServerStorage")

--------------------------------------------------------------
-- 1. LOAD SHARED CONFIG
--------------------------------------------------------------
-- GunConfig must be in ReplicatedStorage so both server & client can read it
local GunConfig = require(ReplicatedStorage:WaitForChild("GunConfig"))

--------------------------------------------------------------
-- 2. CREATE REMOTE EVENTS
--------------------------------------------------------------
-- We create them here so the server is the authority on their existence.
-- FireGun: Client -> Server (player wants to shoot)
-- HitEffect: Server -> All Clients (show impact FX at a world position)
-- ReplicateShot: Server -> All Clients (show muzzle flash / tracer for other players)

local function createRemoteEvent(name: string): RemoteEvent
    local existing = ReplicatedStorage:FindFirstChild(name)
    if existing then
        existing:Destroy() -- clean slate on server restart
    end
    local remote = Instance.new("RemoteEvent")
    remote.Name = name
    remote.Parent = ReplicatedStorage
    return remote
end

local fireGunRemote = createRemoteEvent("FireGun")
local hitEffectRemote = createRemoteEvent("HitEffect")
local replicateShotRemote = createRemoteEvent("ReplicateShot")

--------------------------------------------------------------
-- 3. PER-PLAYER STATE TRACKING
--------------------------------------------------------------
-- Tracks cooldowns and ammo on the server to prevent exploits.
-- The client also tracks ammo for UI, but the server is the
-- final authority.

-- Type: { [Player]: { lastFireTime: number, ammo: number, isReloading: boolean } }
local playerGunState: { [Player]: { lastFireTime: number, amm

tasks.name: GunClient
tasks.description: Client LocalScript: handle mouse input, fire rate limiting, ammo tracking, reload keybind, send fire remote, play local FX (muzzle flash, tracer, impact), update ammo HUD.
tasks.result:
# Task 3: GunClient — The Client-Side Brain

This is where the magic happens on the player's screen. This LocalScript handles everything the player *feels* — input detection, fire rate throttling, ammo management, reload logic, and all the visual/audio feedback (muzzle flash, bullet tracers, impact effects). It communicates with the server via RemoteEvents but never trusts itself with damage — that's the server's job.

**Key design decisions:**
- **Client-side prediction** for instant feedback (FX play immediately, server validates the actual hit)
- **Ammo is tracked client-side** for responsive UI, but the server independently validates fire rate
- **Muzzle flash** uses a temporary `PointLight` + small `Part` that gets cleaned up fast
- **Tracers** are thin parts that tween from barrel to hit point then destroy
- **Impact effects** use `ParticleEmitter` bursts on a temporary attachment
- **Reload** locks input and plays a visual indicator via the HUD (Task 4 will build the full GUI, but we prep the hooks here)

Sits in `StarterPlayerScripts` or inside the Gun `Tool` itself. This version is designed to go **inside the Tool** as a child LocalScript.

```lua
FILENAME: GunClient.lua

--[[
	GunClient.lua
	Location: StarterPack > GunTool > GunClient (LocalScript)
	
	Client-side gun controller. Handles:
	- Mouse input (hold to auto-fire or click for semi)
	- Fire rate limiting
	- Ammo tracking & reload (R key)
	- Sending fire requests to server
	- Local FX: muzzle flash, tracer, impact particles
	- Ammo HUD updates (hooks into AmmoHUD ScreenGui)
	
	Depends on:
	- GunConfig (ReplicatedStorage.GunConfig)
	- RemoteEvents: GunFire, GunHitEffect (ReplicatedStorage)
]]

-- ============================================================
-- SERVICES
-- ============================================================
local Players = game:GetService("Players")
local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")
local UserInputService = game:GetService("UserInputService")
local RunService = game:GetService("RunService")
local TweenService = game:GetService("TweenService")
local Debris = game:GetService("Debris")

-- ============================================================
-- REFERENCES
-- ============================================================
local player = Players.LocalPlayer
local mouse = player:GetMouse()
local camera = workspace.CurrentCamera

-- Tool reference (this script lives inside the Tool)
local tool = script.Parent
local handle = tool:WaitForChild("Handle")

-- Config module
local GunConfig = require(ReplicatedStorage:WaitForChild("GunConfig"))

-- Remote events
local gunFireRemote = ReplicatedStorage:WaitForChild("GunFire") -- client -> server fire request
local gunHitEffectRemote = ReplicatedStorage:WaitForChild("GunHitEffect") -- server -> all clients for hit FX

-- ============================================================
-- STATE VARIABLES
-- ============================================================
local currentAmmo: number

tasks.name: AmmoHUD
tasks.description: ScreenGui with ammo counter, reload indicator, and crosshair. Clean minimal UI created entirely via code.
tasks.result:
# Task 4: AmmoHUD

Alright, let's build a clean, minimal, and actually *good-looking* ammo HUD entirely through code. We're talking ammo counter, reload indicator with animated progress, and a crisp crosshair — no ScreenGui assets needed, pure Luau craftsmanship.

This module will be required by the `GunClient` to create, update, and destroy the HUD. It exposes simple methods so the client script stays clean.

---

FILENAME: AmmoHUD.lua

```lua
--[[
	AmmoHUD.lua
	Location: ReplicatedStorage/Gun/AmmoHUD

	Clean minimal ammo HUD created entirely via code.
	Provides:
		- Ammo counter (current / max) with low-ammo warning
		- Reload progress bar with animated fill
		- Centered crosshair (dot + lines style)
		- Hit marker flash on successful hits

	Required by GunClient. All UI elements are built and
	managed through this module's API.

	Usage:
		local AmmoHUD = require(ReplicatedStorage.Gun.AmmoHUD)
		AmmoHUD:Create()
		AmmoHUD:UpdateAmmo(currentAmmo, maxAmmo)
		AmmoHUD:ShowReloading(reloadTime)
		AmmoHUD:ShowHitMarker()
		AmmoHUD:SetCrosshairVisible(bool)
		AmmoHUD:Destroy()
--]]

local Players = game:GetService("Players")
local TweenService = game:GetService("TweenService")
local RunService = game:GetService("RunService")

local localPlayer = Players.LocalPlayer
local playerGui = localPlayer:WaitForChild("PlayerGui")

-- ============================================================
-- MODULE
-- ============================================================
local AmmoHUD = {}
AmmoHUD.__index = AmmoHUD

-- ============================================================
-- CONFIGURATION
-- ============================================================
local CONFIG = {
	-- Colors
	backgroundColor = Color3.fromRGB(20, 20, 20),
	panelColor = Color3.fromRGB(30, 30, 30),
	accentColor = Color3.fromRGB(0, 200, 120),       -- green accent
	warningColor = Color3.fromRGB(255, 80, 60),       -- low ammo red
	criticalColor = Color3.fromRGB(255, 40, 40),      -- very low ammo
	textColor = Color3.fromRGB(240, 240, 240),
	dimTextColor = Color3.fromRGB(160, 160, 160),
	crosshairColor = Color3.fromRGB(255, 255, 255),
	hitMarkerColor = Color3.fromRGB(255, 60, 60),
	reloadBarBg = Color3.fromRGB(50, 50, 50),
	reloadBarFill = Color3.fromRGB(0, 200, 120),

	-- Thresholds (fraction of max ammo)
	lowAmmoThreshold = 0.3,    -- 30% = warning color
	criticalAmmoThreshold = 0.15, -- 15% = critical color + pulse

	-- Crosshair
	crosshairSize = 20,        -- overall radius in pixels
	crosshairGap = 6,          -- gap from center
	crosshairThickness = 2,
	crosshairDotSize = 4,

	-- Hit marker
	hitMarkerSize = 18,
	hitMarkerThickness = 2,
	hitMarkerDuration = 0.25,

	-- Fonts
	ammoFont = Enum.Font.GothamBold,
	labelFont = Enum.Font.GothamMedium,
}

-- ============================================================
-- INTERNAL STATE
-- ============================================================

local ammoLabel = nil        -- TextLabel for "

tasks.name: GunToolSetup
tasks.description: Script that builds the gun Tool instance with a blocky but good-looking gun model (handle, barrel, stock, sight, magazine) using Parts and Welds, all created via code. Inserts into StarterPack.
tasks.result:
# Task 5: GunToolSetup — Building the Gun Tool from Pure Code

Alright, this is the final piece of the puzzle — the actual physical gun that players hold. We're building a **blocky, stylized assault rifle** entirely from code. No meshes, no imported models — just Parts, Welds, and clean geometry that looks *crisp*.

**What this script does:**
- Creates a `Tool` with `RequiresHandle = true`
- Builds a multi-part gun model: **Handle (grip), Body (receiver), Barrel, Stock, Magazine, Sight (front + rear posts), and a Muzzle attachment point**
- Welds every part to the Handle so it moves as one unit
- Colors everything with a tactical dark palette
- Adds the `GunClient` LocalScript and `AmmoHUD` LocalScript as children of the Tool
- Drops the finished Tool into `StarterPack` so every player spawns with it
- This is a **server Script** — runs once on startup

The muzzle point is an `Attachment` named `MuzzlePoint` on the barrel tip — your GunClient should reference this for muzzle flash and tracer origin.

```lua
FILENAME: GunToolSetup.lua
-- GunToolSetup.lua
-- Server Script | Location: ServerScriptService
-- Builds the entire gun Tool from code: blocky tactical rifle with proper welds.
-- Inserts into StarterPack so every player spawns with it.

--[[
    GUN ANATOMY (side view, not to scale):
    
        [Rear Sight]                [Front Sight]
            |                           |
    [Stock]=====[  Body / Receiver  ]===[Barrel]-->  (MuzzlePoint)
                      |
                   [Handle/Grip]
                      |
                  [Magazine]
                  
    All parts welded to Handle. Tool.GripPos adjusted so it holds naturally.
]]

-- ============================================================
-- SERVICES
-- ============================================================

local StarterPack = game:GetService("StarterPack")
local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")

-- ============================================================
-- HELPER FUNCTIONS
-- ============================================================

--- Creates a Part with given properties and parents it
--- @param name string - Part name
--- @param size Vector3 - Part size
--- @param color Color3 - Part color
--- @param parent Instance - Parent instance
--- @return BasePart
local function createPart(name: string, size: Vector3, color: Color3, parent: Instance): BasePart
	local part = Instance.new("Part")
	part.Name = name
	part.Size = size
	part.Color = color
	part.Material = Enum.Material.SmoothPlastic
	part.Anchored = false
	part.CanCollide = false
	part.Massless = true -- So the gun doesn't weigh the character down
	part.TopSurface = Enum.SurfaceType.Smooth
	part.BottomSurface = Enum.SurfaceType.Smooth
	part.Parent = parent
	return part
end

--- Creates a WeldConstraint between two parts
--- @param part0 BasePart - First part (usually the Handle)
--- @param part1 BasePart - Second part to weld to part0
--- @param parent Instance - Where
